sentence: Profit-share structures (358) implement independent P&L accountability (25) by aligning GM compensation to location performance.
Organizational decentralization (25) operationalizes outcome-based work design (24) by pushing P&L ownership to location managers.
Both decentralization (25) and offshore leverage (73) scale throughput per founder dollar, using different axes: autonomy/structure vs. cost arbitrage.
Founder accountability liability expands (114) as delegation increases, which organizational decentralization (25) addresses by distributing decision-making authority.
Non-dilutive ownership incentives (80) align employee thinking with decentralized P&L ownership (25) without transferring voting control.
Partnership value test (43) resolves founder bottlenecks (30) by filling capability gaps the founder cannot address independently.
Both partnership viability (43) and conviction-under-isolation (44) test whether a founder has intrinsic capability or relies on external validation.
Partnership value (43) differs from founder-led differentiation (102): partnerships require capability gaps while underdog differentiation relies on founder accessibility.
Founder-led executive recruiting (72) demonstrates the capability to attract talent that validates partnership value (43).
Scarcity as value driver (184) motivates partnerships (43) by creating constraints that justify filling capability gaps.
Both founder character (38) and partnership value test (43) assert that strategic alliances require the founder's demonstrated credibility to succeed.
Founder-dependent operations resist scaling (357) because partnerships fail the viability test (43) when founders cannot be removed from critical paths.
Partnership value test (43) and founder removal test (150) both measure whether a business can survive without founder involvement.
Partnership value (43) requires capability gaps that compound work payoff (116) does not—one seeks external solutions, the other builds internal mastery.
Team capacity bottlenecks (191) are resolved by partnerships (43) that fill execution gaps the founder cannot solo.
Partnership value test (43) assumes capability gaps can be filled; avatar mismatch (270) shows some founders lack capability to activate partners' solutions.
Both founder conviction under isolation (44) and founder removal test (150) measure whether a business survives when the founder exits.
Conviction under isolation (44) predicts founder-led differentiation (102); founders with intrinsic conviction sustain underdog advantage longer.
Founder bottleneck (30) is exacerbated by conviction under isolation (44); founders who depend on isolation may resist delegation needed for scale.
Founder resilience narrative (162) is forged during periods of conviction under isolation (44) via catastrophic loss or constrained escape.
Founder-led executive recruiting (72) succeeds when conviction is intrinsic (44); founders with proven resilience attract top talent.
Contrarian conviction (328) is tested by isolation (44); outsized returns accrue to founders whose bets survive without external validation.
Growth path selection (39) determines whether conviction (44) must survive in isolation; organic paths require intrinsic founder strength.
Both founder conviction under isolation (44) and early-stage identity construction (79) require shifting from external validation to internal motivation.
Founder character (38) predicts performance during isolation (44); reliability and vision are tested by lack of peer support or market validation.
Business leverage through founder dependency (45) emerges when conviction under isolation (44) creates founder-specific value that competitors cannot replicate.
Both founder dependency as leverage (45) and founder-dependent operations resist scaling (357) observe the same constraint: founder-centric businesses fail to scale.
Founder bottleneck (30) and business leverage through founder dependency (45) describe the same phenomenon from opposite perspectives: constraint vs. asset.
Both founder-dependent business leverage (45) and founder removal test (150) measure: what breaks if the founder exits?
Founder-led differentiation (102) creates founder dependency (45); high-touch accessibility that differentiates early-stage becomes a scaling trap.
Founder resilience (162) correlates with dependency (45); catastrophic experience creates founder-specific operating psychology that becomes hard to replicate.
Single-channel domination (260) resembles founder dependency (45): both concentrate value in one irreplaceable element (founder or channel).
Opportunity cost of finite seasons (189) is amplified by founder dependency (45); founder must choose which seasons to allocate without delegation.
Founder-led recruiting (72) perpetuates dependency (45); founder's talent judgment is irreplaceable, creating staffing bottleneck.
Both founder dependency as leverage (45) and leverage mechanics (139) define success: identify the irreplaceable input (founder action) that drives business results.
Avatar mismatch (270) exploits founder dependency (45); non-sales-capable founders cannot execute acquisition services even if procured from partners.
Founder control and equity structure (138) protects against dilution caused by founder dependency (45); voting control prevents replacement during scaling.
Founder performance decay (46) precipitates equity dilution (115); investor dilution occurs when replacement cost exceeds founder premium.
Founder bottleneck (30) and founder performance decay (46) describe the same scaling challenge: founder outperformance by ~30% but is not replaceable at that level.
Founder-led recruiting (72) documents founder performance standards (46); recruiting demonstrates the 30% premium to be absorbed at founder exit.
Founder-led differentiation (102) decays (46) as the founder becomes a scaling liability; early advantage becomes obsolescence.
Founder delegation sequence (112) manages performance decay (46): document first, train second, delegate third to absorb the 30% margin loss.
Churn masks acquisition inefficiency (271) when founder performance decay (46) is hidden by replacement hires who underdeliver.
Both founder performance decay (46) and founder removal test (150) measure what breaks when the founder exits: ~30% performance margin is the expected loss.
Customer retention mechanics (179) show churn patterns that reveal founder performance decay (46): month-1 spikes reflect loss of founder touch.
Control paradox (113) explains performance decay (46): founder control that enabled early success is precisely what cannot transfer.
Sales scaling (129) requires overcoming founder performance decay (46); trainer-multiplier model breaks founder's direct involvement in closes.
Cohort-based churn analysis (146) isolates founder performance decay (46): month-6 inflection reveals founder touch loss in customer success.
Founder resilience (162) correlates negatively with performance decay (46); trauma shapes operating patterns but does not make founder performance transferable.
Both supply constraint levers (47) and capacity maximization (217) achieve the same goal: serve more clients without proportional hiring via pricing, productization, and process.
Supply constraint levers (47) and supply vs. demand diagnosis (118) both measure and resolve capacity limits; diagnosis determines which lever to pull.
Opportunity cost of finite seasons (189) determines supply constraint lever choice (47); founder must allocate finite seasons to scale capacity, not create new capacity.
